{{Blockquote|text=Those virtuous kami who care for the land are called kunitama or kunimitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />|author={{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}}}}

== Outside of Japan ==
A generic "Kunitama" was among the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name="koji-2010a">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53–54}} They are all [[Kunitsukami]] or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

This started in the [[Matsumae Domain]] during ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'' where many shrines in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] were forced to adopt such deities in that group. There was very little worship of such deities there at that time and as a result not much objection to it.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} This came to be later used in many overseas shrines to justify colonialism.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

In Korea Kunitama and Amaterasu were enshrined together.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|126}} as a pair at all nationally Ranked shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|139}} The colonization of Korea marked the beginning of a shift frrom a meiji era "pioneer theology" to a universal theology and Amaterasu became more prominent and was generally paired with Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|217}}

=== In Korea ===
Some people identified [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the government not wanting to take a stand on this enshrined the generic Okunitama at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] so believers could have their own interpretations.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}} {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} was a strong advocate of these positions and his advocacy was associated with the enshrinement of Okunitama at both [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]], and [[Keijō Shrine]].<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|56}} He advocated enshrining of [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] at {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine}}, and others argued that in Korea Kunitama was Dangun and should be called Chosen kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|132}}

In 1936 [[Keijō Shrine]] released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title forr any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|140}}

An ethnic Korean group proposed to take over Okunitama worship after the war but was denied.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|57}}

State authorities at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] however never allowed for Okunitama to be called "Chosen kunitama" and indigenous Dangun traditions were suppressed in favor of worshipping Amaterasu in the shrine.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}}

=== Other areas ===
In {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}} there were proposals to identify Kunitama with {{ill|Nurhaci|en|Nurhaci|WD=Q311189|lt=Nurhaci}} but they were not accepted.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|161}}

At Mōkyō Jinja {{ill|Genghis Khan|en|Genghis Khan|WD=Q720|lt=Genghis Khan}} was venerated as Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|175}}

In {{ill|Brazil|en|Brazil|WD=Q155|lt=Brazil}} in a Japanese settlement a shrine named Bogure Jinja was created and worshipped Kunitama, identified with indigenous people of the area in a Kofun.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|209}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Japanese Category:kk]]

[[Category of deities}}

'''Kunitama''' (国魂)  is a type of [[kami]] or god who acts as a {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity}} or guardian of a [[:en:province of Japan|province of Japan]] or sometimes other areas in Shinto.<ref name="nishioka-2">{{cite web |last=Nishioka |first=Kazuhiko |title=Kunitama |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8983 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref><ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|102}}

The term is sometimes treated as a specific deity itself especially with [[Hokkaidō Jingu|Hokkaidō Shrine]],<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} and other colonial shrines,<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|217}} a or as an epithet in the case of [[Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]]<ref name="nelson-1994a" /> or a part of a deity's name in the case of {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama}}, whose name is also sometimes interpreted as an epithet.<ref name="ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

== History ==
In ancient times it was believed that every province had a kunitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />

{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama}} is the Kunitama of {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}. He is sometimes identified with [[Ōmononushi]].<ref name="ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}}

As the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}} grew in power shrines were made in more and more places outside of the Yamato region.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

{{ill|Kunitama|en|Kunitama|WD=Q9692242|lt=Musahi no Okunitama}} of the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}} was traditionally identified as [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="nelson-1994a" />

[[Hirata Atsutane]] said in his morning prayers that the deities to worship in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} were [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Okunitama}}, and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]].<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|343}}

{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}} discussed the concept.<ref name="nishioka-2" />
{{Blockquote|text=Those virtuous kami who care for the land are called kunitama or kunimitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />|author={{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}}}}

== Outside of Japan ==
A generic "Kunitama" was among the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name="koji-2010a">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53–54}} They are all [[Kunitsukami]] or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

This started in the [[Matsumae Domain]] during ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'' where many shrines in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] were forced to adopt such deities in that group. There was very little worship of such deities there at that time and as a result not much objection to it.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} This came to be later used in many overseas shrines to justify colonialism.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

In Korea Kunitama and Amaterasu were enshrined together.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|126}} as a pair at all nationally Ranked shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|139}} The colonization of Korea marked the beginning of a shift frrom a meiji era "pioneer theology" to a universal theology and Amaterasu became more prominent and was generally paired with Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|217}}

=== In Korea ===
Some people identified [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the government not wanting to take a stand on this enshrined the generic Okunitama at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] so believers could have their own interpretations.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}} {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} was a strong advocate of these positions and his advocacy was associated with the enshrinement of Okunitama at both [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]], and [[Keijō Shrine]].<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|56}} He advocated enshrining of [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] at {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine}}, and others argued that in Korea Kunitama was Dangun and should be called Chosen kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|132}}

In 1936 [[Keijō Shrine]] released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title forr any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|140}}

An ethnic Korean group proposed to take over Okunitama worship after the war but was denied.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|57}}

State authorities at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] however never allowed for Okunitama to be called "Chosen kunitama" and indigenous Dangun traditions were suppressed in favor of worshipping Amaterasu in the shrine.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}}

=== Other areas ===
In {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}} there were proposals to identify Kunitama with {{ill|Nurhaci|en|Nurhaci|WD=Q311189|lt=Nurhaci}} but they were not accepted.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|161}}

At Mōkyō Jinja {{ill|Genghis Khan|en|Genghis Khan|WD=Q720|lt=Genghis Khan}} was venerated as Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|175}}

In {{ill|Brazil|en|Brazil|WD=Q155|lt=Brazil}} in a Japanese settlement a shrine named Bogure Jinja was created and worshipped Kunitama, identified with indigenous people of the area in a Kofun.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|209}}

{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}} discussed the concept.<ref name="nishioka-2" />
{{Blockquote|text=Those virtuous kami who care for the land are called kunitama or kunimitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />|author={{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}}}}

== Outside of Japan ==
A generic "Kunitama" was among the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name="koji-2010a">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53–54}} They are all [[Kunitsukami]] or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

This started in the [[Matsumae Domain]] during ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'' where many shrines in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] were forced to adopt such deities in that group. There was very little worship of such deities there at that time and as a result not much objection to it.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} This came to be later used in many overseas shrines to justify colonialism.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

In Korea Kunitama and Amaterasu were enshrined together.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|126}} as a pair at all nationally Ranked shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|139}} The colonization of Korea marked the beginning of a shift frrom a meiji era "pioneer theology" to a universal theology and Amaterasu became more prominent and was generally paired with Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|217}}

=== In Korea ===
Some people identified [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the government not wanting to take a stand on this enshrined the generic Okunitama at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] so believers could have their own interpretations.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}} {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} was a strong advocate of these positions and his advocacy was associated with the enshrinement of Okunitama at both [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]], and [[Keijō Shrine]].<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|56}} He advocated enshrining of [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] at {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine}}, and others argued that in Korea Kunitama was Dangun and should be called Chosen kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|132}}

In 1936 [[Keijō Shrine]] released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title forr any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|140}}

An ethnic Korean group proposed to take over Okunitama worship after the war but was denied.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|57}}

State authorities at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] however never allowed for Okunitama to be called "Chosen kunitama" and indigenous Dangun traditions were suppressed in favor of worshipping Amaterasu in the shrine.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}}

=== Other areas ===
In {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}} there were proposals to identify Kunitama with {{ill|Nurhaci|en|Nurhaci|WD=Q311189|lt=Nurhaci}} but they were not accepted.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|161}}

At Mōkyō Jinja {{ill|Genghis Khan|en|Genghis Khan|WD=Q720|lt=Genghis Khan}} was venerated as Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|175}}

In {{ill|Brazil|en|Brazil|WD=Q155|lt=Brazil}} in a Japanese settlement a shrine named Bogure Jinja was created and worshipped Kunitama, identified with indigenous people of the area in a Kofun.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|209}}

{{Short description|Japanese Category:kk]]

[[Category of deities}}

'''Kunitama''' (国魂)  is a type of [[kami]] or god who acts as a {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity}} or guardian of a [[:en:province of Japan|province of Japan]] or sometimes other areas in Shinto.<ref name="nishioka-2">{{cite web |last=Nishioka |first=Kazuhiko |title=Kunitama |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8983 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref><ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|102}}

The term is sometimes treated as a specific deity itself especially with [[Hokkaidō Jingu|Hokkaidō Shrine]],<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} and other colonial shrines,<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|217}} a or as an epithet in the case of [[Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]]<ref name="nelson-1994a" /> or a part of a deity's name in the case of {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama}}, whose name is also sometimes interpreted as an epithet.<ref name="ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

== History ==
In ancient times it was believed that every province had a kunitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />

{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama}} is the Kunitama of {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}. He is sometimes identified with [[Ōmononushi]].<ref name="ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}}

As the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}} grew in power shrines were made in more and more places outside of the Yamato region.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

{{ill|Kunitama|en|Kunitama|WD=Q9692242|lt=Musahi no Okunitama}} of the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}} was traditionally identified as [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="nelson-1994a" />

[[Hirata Atsutane]] said in his morning prayers that the deities to worship in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} were [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Okunitama}}, and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]].<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|343}}

{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}} discussed the concept.<ref name="nishioka-2" />
{{Blockquote|text=Those virtuous kami who care for the land are called kunitama or kunimitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />|author={{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}}}}

== Outside of Japan ==
A generic "Kunitama" was among the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name="koji-2010a">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53–54}} They are all [[Kunitsukami]] or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

This started in the [[Matsumae Domain]] during ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'' where many shrines in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] were forced to adopt such deities in that group. There was very little worship of such deities there at that time and as a result not much objection to it.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} This came to be later used in many overseas shrines to justify colonialism.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

In Korea Kunitama and Amaterasu were enshrined together.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|126}} as a pair at all nationally Ranked shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|139}} The colonization of Korea marked the beginning of a shift frrom a meiji era "pioneer theology" to a universal theology and Amaterasu became more prominent and was generally paired with Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|217}}

=== In Korea ===
Some people identified [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the government not wanting to take a stand on this enshrined the generic Okunitama at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] so believers could have their own interpretations.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}} {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} was a strong advocate of these positions and his advocacy was associated with the enshrinement of Okunitama at both [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]], and [[Keijō Shrine]].<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|56}} He advocated enshrining of [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] at {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine}}, and others argued that in Korea Kunitama was Dangun and should be called Chosen kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|132}}

In 1936 [[Keijō Shrine]] released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title forr any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|140}}

An ethnic Korean group proposed to take over Okunitama worship after the war but was denied.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|57}}

State authorities at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] however never allowed for Okunitama to be called "Chosen kunitama" and indigenous Dangun traditions were suppressed in favor of worshipping Amaterasu in the shrine.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}}

=== Other areas ===
In {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}} there were proposals to identify Kunitama with {{ill|Nurhaci|en|Nurhaci|WD=Q311189|lt=Nurhaci}} but they were not accepted.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|161}}

At Mōkyō Jinja {{ill|Genghis Khan|en|Genghis Khan|WD=Q720|lt=Genghis Khan}} was venerated as Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|175}}

In {{ill|Brazil|en|Brazil|WD=Q155|lt=Brazil}} in a Japanese settlement a shrine named Bogure Jinja was created and worshipped Kunitama, identified with indigenous people of the area in a Kofun.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|209}}

{{Blockquote|text=Those virtuous kami who care for the land are called kunitama or kunimitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />|author={{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}}}}

== Outside of Japan ==
A generic "Kunitama" was among the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name="koji-2010a">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53–54}} They are all [[Kunitsukami]] or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

This started in the [[Matsumae Domain]] during ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'' where many shrines in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] were forced to adopt such deities in that group. There was very little worship of such deities there at that time and as a result not much objection to it.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} This came to be later used in many overseas shrines to justify colonialism.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

In Korea Kunitama and Amaterasu were enshrined together.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|126}} as a pair at all nationally Ranked shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|139}} The colonization of Korea marked the beginning of a shift frrom a meiji era "pioneer theology" to a universal theology and Amaterasu became more prominent and was generally paired with Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|217}}

=== In Korea ===
Some people identified [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the government not wanting to take a stand on this enshrined the generic Okunitama at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] so believers could have their own interpretations.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}} {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} was a strong advocate of these positions and his advocacy was associated with the enshrinement of Okunitama at both [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]], and [[Keijō Shrine]].<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|56}} He advocated enshrining of [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] at {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine}}, and others argued that in Korea Kunitama was Dangun and should be called Chosen kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|132}}

In 1936 [[Keijō Shrine]] released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title forr any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|140}}

An ethnic Korean group proposed to take over Okunitama worship after the war but was denied.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|57}}

State authorities at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] however never allowed for Okunitama to be called "Chosen kunitama" and indigenous Dangun traditions were suppressed in favor of worshipping Amaterasu in the shrine.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}}

=== Other areas ===
In {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}} there were proposals to identify Kunitama with {{ill|Nurhaci|en|Nurhaci|WD=Q311189|lt=Nurhaci}} but they were not accepted.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|161}}

At Mōkyō Jinja {{ill|Genghis Khan|en|Genghis Khan|WD=Q720|lt=Genghis Khan}} was venerated as Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|175}}

In {{ill|Brazil|en|Brazil|WD=Q155|lt=Brazil}} in a Japanese settlement a shrine named Bogure Jinja was created and worshipped Kunitama, identified with indigenous people of the area in a Kofun.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|209}}

{{Short description|Japanese Category:kk]]

[[Category of deities}}

'''Kunitama''' (国魂)  is a type of [[kami]] or god who acts as a {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity}} or guardian of a [[:en:province of Japan|province of Japan]] or sometimes other areas in Shinto.<ref name="nishioka-2">{{cite web |last=Nishioka |first=Kazuhiko |title=Kunitama |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8983 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref><ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|102}}

The term is sometimes treated as a specific deity itself especially with [[Hokkaidō Jingu|Hokkaidō Shrine]],<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} and other colonial shrines,<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|217}} a or as an epithet in the case of [[Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]]<ref name="nelson-1994a" /> or a part of a deity's name in the case of {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama}}, whose name is also sometimes interpreted as an epithet.<ref name="ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

== History ==
In ancient times it was believed that every province had a kunitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />

{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama}} is the Kunitama of {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}. He is sometimes identified with [[Ōmononushi]].<ref name="ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}}

As the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}} grew in power shrines were made in more and more places outside of the Yamato region.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

{{ill|Kunitama|en|Kunitama|WD=Q9692242|lt=Musahi no Okunitama}} of the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}} was traditionally identified as [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="nelson-1994a" />

[[Hirata Atsutane]] said in his morning prayers that the deities to worship in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} were [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Okunitama}}, and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]].<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|343}}

{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}} discussed the concept.<ref name="nishioka-2" />
{{Blockquote|text=Those virtuous kami who care for the land are called kunitama or kunimitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />|author={{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}}}}

== Outside of Japan ==
A generic "Kunitama" was among the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name="koji-2010a">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53–54}} They are all [[Kunitsukami]] or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

This started in the [[Matsumae Domain]] during ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'' where many shrines in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] were forced to adopt such deities in that group. There was very little worship of such deities there at that time and as a result not much objection to it.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} This came to be later used in many overseas shrines to justify colonialism.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

In Korea Kunitama and Amaterasu were enshrined together.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|126}} as a pair at all nationally Ranked shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|139}} The colonization of Korea marked the beginning of a shift frrom a meiji era "pioneer theology" to a universal theology and Amaterasu became more prominent and was generally paired with Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|217}}

=== In Korea ===
Some people identified [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the government not wanting to take a stand on this enshrined the generic Okunitama at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] so believers could have their own interpretations.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}} {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} was a strong advocate of these positions and his advocacy was associated with the enshrinement of Okunitama at both [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]], and [[Keijō Shrine]].<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|56}} He advocated enshrining of [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] at {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine}}, and others argued that in Korea Kunitama was Dangun and should be called Chosen kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|132}}

In 1936 [[Keijō Shrine]] released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title forr any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|140}}

An ethnic Korean group proposed to take over Okunitama worship after the war but was denied.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|57}}

State authorities at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] however never allowed for Okunitama to be called "Chosen kunitama" and indigenous Dangun traditions were suppressed in favor of worshipping Amaterasu in the shrine.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}}

=== Other areas ===
In {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}} there were proposals to identify Kunitama with {{ill|Nurhaci|en|Nurhaci|WD=Q311189|lt=Nurhaci}} but they were not accepted.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|161}}

At Mōkyō Jinja {{ill|Genghis Khan|en|Genghis Khan|WD=Q720|lt=Genghis Khan}} was venerated as Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|175}}

In {{ill|Brazil|en|Brazil|WD=Q155|lt=Brazil}} in a Japanese settlement a shrine named Bogure Jinja was created and worshipped Kunitama, identified with indigenous people of the area in a Kofun.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|209}}

{{Blockquote|text=Those virtuous kami who care for the land are called kunitama or kunimitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />|author={{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}}}

== Outside of Japan ==
A generic "Kunitama" was among the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name="koji-2010a">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53–54}} They are all [[Kunitsukami]] or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

This started in the [[Matsumae Domain]] during ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'' where many shrines in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] were forced to adopt such deities in that group. There was very little worship of such deities there at that time and as a result not much objection to it.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} This came to be later used in many overseas shrines to justify colonialism.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

In Korea Kunitama and Amaterasu were enshrined together.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|126}} as a pair at all nationally Ranked shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|139}} The colonization of Korea marked the beginning of a shift frrom a meiji era "pioneer theology" to a universal theology and Amaterasu became more prominent and was generally paired with Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|217}}

=== In Korea ===
Some people identified [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the government not wanting to take a stand on this enshrined the generic Okunitama at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] so believers could have their own interpretations.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}} {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} was a strong advocate of these positions and his advocacy was associated with the enshrinement of Okunitama at both [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]], and [[Keijō Shrine]].<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|56}} He advocated enshrining of [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] at {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine|1=Chōsen Shrine}}, and others argued that in Korea Kunitama was Dangun and should be called Chosen kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|132}}

In 1936 [[Keijō Shrine]] released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title forr any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|140}}

An ethnic Korean group proposed to take over Okunitama worship after the war but was denied.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|57}}

State authorities at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] however never allowed for Okunitama to be called "Chosen kunitama" and indigenous Dangun traditions were suppressed in favor of worshipping Amaterasu in the shrine.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}}

=== Other areas ===
In {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo|1=Manchukuo}} there were proposals to identify Kunitama with {{ill|Nurhaci|en|Nurhaci|WD=Q311189|lt=Nurhaci|1=Nurhaci}} but they were not accepted.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|161}}

At Mōkyō Jinja {{ill|Genghis Khan|en|Genghis Khan|WD=Q720|lt=Genghis Khan|1=Genghis Khan}} was venerated as Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|175}}

In {{ill|Brazil|en|Brazil|WD=Q155|lt=Brazil|1=Brazil}} in a Japanese settlement a shrine named Bogure Jinja was created and worshipped Kunitama, identified with indigenous people of the area in a Kofun.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|209}}

{{Short description|Japanese Category:kk]]

[[Category of deities}}

'''Kunitama''' (国魂)  is a type of [[kami]] or god who acts as a {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity|1=Tutelary deity}} or guardian of a [[:en:province of Japan|province of Japan]] or sometimes other areas in Shinto.<ref name="nishioka-2">{{cite web |last=Nishioka |first=Kazuhiko |title=Kunitama |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8983 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref><ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|102}}

The term is sometimes treated as a specific deity itself especially with [[Hokkaidō Jingu|Hokkaidō Shrine]],<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} and other colonial shrines,<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|217}} a or as an epithet in the case of [[Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]]<ref name="nelson-1994a" /> or a part of a deity's name in the case of {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, whose name is also sometimes interpreted as an epithet.<ref name="ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

== History ==
In ancient times it was believed that every province had a kunitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />

{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}} is the Kunitama of {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}. He is sometimes identified with [[Ōmononushi]].<ref name="ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}}

As the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}} grew in power shrines were made in more and more places outside of the Yamato region.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

{{ill|Kunitama|en|Kunitama|WD=Q9692242|lt=Musahi no Okunitama|1=Kunitama}} of the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} was traditionally identified as [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="nelson-1994a" />

[[Hirata Atsutane]] said in his morning prayers that the deities to worship in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} were [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]].<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|343}}

{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} discussed the concept.<ref name="nishioka-2" />
{{Blockquote|text=Those virtuous kami who care for the land are called kunitama or kunimitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />|author={{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}}}

== Outside of Japan ==
A generic "Kunitama" was among the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name="koji-2010a">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53–54}} They are all [[Kunitsukami]] or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

This started in the [[Matsumae Domain]] during ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'' where many shrines in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] were forced to adopt such deities in that group. There was very little worship of such deities there at that time and as a result not much objection to it.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} This came to be later used in many overseas shrines to justify colonialism.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

In Korea Kunitama and Amaterasu were enshrined together.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|126}} as a pair at all nationally Ranked shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|139}} The colonization of Korea marked the beginning of a shift frrom a meiji era "pioneer theology" to a universal theology and Amaterasu became more prominent and was generally paired with Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|217}}

=== In Korea ===
Some people identified [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the government not wanting to take a stand on this enshrined the generic Okunitama at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] so believers could have their own interpretations.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}} {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} was a strong advocate of these positions and his advocacy was associated with the enshrinement of Okunitama at both [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]], and [[Keijō Shrine]].<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|56}} He advocated enshrining of [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] at {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine|1=Chōsen Shrine}}, and others argued that in Korea Kunitama was Dangun and should be called Chosen kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|132}}

In 1936 [[Keijō Shrine]] released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title forr any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|140}}

An ethnic Korean group proposed to take over Okunitama worship after the war but was denied.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|57}}

State authorities at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] however never allowed for Okunitama to be called "Chosen kunitama" and indigenous Dangun traditions were suppressed in favor of worshipping Amaterasu in the shrine.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}}

=== Other areas ===
In {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo|1=Manchukuo}} there were proposals to identify Kunitama with {{ill|Nurhaci|en|Nurhaci|WD=Q311189|lt=Nurhaci|1=Nurhaci}} but they were not accepted.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|161}}

At Mōkyō Jinja {{ill|Genghis Khan|en|Genghis Khan|WD=Q720|lt=Genghis Khan|1=Genghis Khan}} was venerated as Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|175}}

In {{ill|Brazil|en|Brazil|WD=Q155|lt=Brazil|1=Brazil}} in a Japanese settlement a shrine named Bogure Jinja was created and worshipped Kunitama, identified with indigenous people of the area in a Kofun.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|209}}

As the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}} grew in power shrines were made in more and more places outside of the Yamato region.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

{{ill|Kunitama|en|Kunitama|WD=Q9692242|lt=Musahi no Okunitama|1=Kunitama}} of the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} was traditionally identified as [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="nelson-1994a" />

[[Hirata Atsutane]] said in his morning prayers that the deities to worship in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} were [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]].<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|343}}

{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} discussed the concept.<ref name="nishioka-2" />
{{Blockquote|text=Those virtuous kami who care for the land are called kunitama or kunimitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />|author={{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}}}

== Outside of Japan ==
A generic "Kunitama" was among the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name="koji-2010a">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53–54}} They are all [[Kunitsukami]] or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

This started in the [[Matsumae Domain]] during ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'' where many shrines in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] were forced to adopt such deities in that group. There was very little worship of such deities there at that time and as a result not much objection to it.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} This came to be later used in many overseas shrines to justify colonialism.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

In Korea Kunitama and Amaterasu were enshrined together.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|126}} as a pair at all nationally Ranked shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|139}} The colonization of Korea marked the beginning of a shift frrom a meiji era "pioneer theology" to a universal theology and Amaterasu became more prominent and was generally paired with Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|217}}

=== In Korea ===
Some people identified [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the government not wanting to take a stand on this enshrined the generic Okunitama at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] so believers could have their own interpretations.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}} {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} was a strong advocate of these positions and his advocacy was associated with the enshrinement of Okunitama at both [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]], and [[Keijō Shrine]].<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|56}} He advocated enshrining of [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] at {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine|1=Chōsen Shrine}}, and others argued that in Korea Kunitama was Dangun and should be called Chosen kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|132}}

In 1936 [[Keijō Shrine]] released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title forr any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|140}}

An ethnic Korean group proposed to take over Okunitama worship after the war but was denied.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|57}}

State authorities at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] however never allowed for Okunitama to be called "Chosen kunitama" and indigenous Dangun traditions were suppressed in favor of worshipping Amaterasu in the shrine.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}}

=== Other areas ===
In {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo|1=Manchukuo}} there were proposals to identify Kunitama with {{ill|Nurhaci|en|Nurhaci|WD=Q311189|lt=Nurhaci|1=Nurhaci}} but they were not accepted.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|161}}

At Mōkyō Jinja {{ill|Genghis Khan|en|Genghis Khan|WD=Q720|lt=Genghis Khan|1=Genghis Khan}} was venerated as Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|175}}

In {{ill|Brazil|en|Brazil|WD=Q155|lt=Brazil|1=Brazil}} in a Japanese settlement a shrine named Bogure Jinja was created and worshipped Kunitama, identified with indigenous people of the area in a Kofun.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|209}}

{{Short description|Japanese Category:kk]]

[[Category of deities}}

'''Kunitama''' (国魂)  is a type of [[kami]] or god who acts as a {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity|1=Tutelary deity}} or guardian of a [[:en:province of Japan|province of Japan]] or sometimes other areas in Shinto.<ref name="nishioka-2">{{cite web |last=Nishioka |first=Kazuhiko |title=Kunitama |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8983 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref><ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|102}}

The term is sometimes treated as a specific deity itself especially with [[Hokkaidō Jingu|Hokkaidō Shrine]],<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} and other colonial shrines,<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|217}} a or as an epithet in the case of [[Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]]<ref name="nelson-1994a" /> or a part of a deity's name in the case of {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, whose name is also sometimes interpreted as an epithet.<ref name="ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

== History ==
In ancient times it was believed that every province had a kunitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />

{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}} is the Kunitama of {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}. He is sometimes identified with [[Ōmononushi]].<ref name="ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}}

As the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}} grew in power shrines were made in more and more places outside of the Yamato region.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

{{ill|Kunitama|en|Kunitama|WD=Q9692242|lt=Musahi no Okunitama|1=Kunitama}} of the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} was traditionally identified as [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="nelson-1994a" />

[[Hirata Atsutane]] said in his morning prayers that the deities to worship in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} were [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]].<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|343}}

{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} discussed the concept.<ref name="nishioka-2" />
{{Blockquote|text=Those virtuous kami who care for the land are called kunitama or kunimitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />|author={{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}}}

== Outside of Japan ==
A generic "Kunitama" was among the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name="koji-2010a">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53–54}} They are all [[Kunitsukami]] or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

This started in the [[Matsumae Domain]] during ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'' where many shrines in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] were forced to adopt such deities in that group. There was very little worship of such deities there at that time and as a result not much objection to it.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} This came to be later used in many overseas shrines to justify colonialism.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

In Korea Kunitama and Amaterasu were enshrined together.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|126}} as a pair at all nationally Ranked shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|139}} The colonization of Korea marked the beginning of a shift frrom a meiji era "pioneer theology" to a universal theology and Amaterasu became more prominent and was generally paired with Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|217}}

=== In Korea ===
Some people identified [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the government not wanting to take a stand on this enshrined the generic Okunitama at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] so believers could have their own interpretations.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}} {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} was a strong advocate of these positions and his advocacy was associated with the enshrinement of Okunitama at both [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]], and [[Keijō Shrine]].<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|56}} He advocated enshrining of [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] at {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine|1=Chōsen Shrine}}, and others argued that in Korea Kunitama was Dangun and should be called Chosen kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|132}}

In 1936 [[Keijō Shrine]] released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title forr any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|140}}

An ethnic Korean group proposed to take over Okunitama worship after the war but was denied.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|57}}

State authorities at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] however never allowed for Okunitama to be called "Chosen kunitama" and indigenous Dangun traditions were suppressed in favor of worshipping Amaterasu in the shrine.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}}

=== Other areas ===
In {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo|1=Manchukuo}} there were proposals to identify Kunitama with {{ill|Nurhaci|en|Nurhaci|WD=Q311189|lt=Nurhaci|1=Nurhaci}} but they were not accepted.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|161}}

At Mōkyō Jinja {{ill|Genghis Khan|en|Genghis Khan|WD=Q720|lt=Genghis Khan|1=Genghis Khan}} was venerated as Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|175}}

In {{ill|Brazil|en|Brazil|WD=Q155|lt=Brazil|1=Brazil}} in a Japanese settlement a shrine named Bogure Jinja was created and worshipped Kunitama, identified with indigenous people of the area in a Kofun.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|209}}

The term is sometimes treated as a specific deity itself especially with [[Hokkaidō Jingu|Hokkaidō Shrine]],<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} and other colonial shrines,<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|217}} a or as an epithet in the case of [[Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]]<ref name="nelson-1994a" /> or a part of a deity's name in the case of {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, whose name is also sometimes interpreted as an epithet.<ref name="ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

== History ==
In ancient times it was believed that every province had a kunitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />

{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Yamato Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}} is the Kunitama of {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}. He is sometimes identified with [[Ōmononushi]].<ref name="ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}}

As the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|qid=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}} grew in power shrines were made in more and more places outside of the Yamato region.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}}

{{ill|Kunitama|en|Kunitama|qid=Q9692242|lt=Musahi no Okunitama|1=Kunitama}} of the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|qid=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} was traditionally identified as [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="nelson-1994a" />

[[Hirata Atsutane]] said in his morning prayers that the deities to worship in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} were [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]].<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|343}}

{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} discussed the concept.<ref name="nishioka-2" />
{{Blockquote|text=Those virtuous kami who care for the land are called kunitama or kunimitama.<ref name="nishioka-2" />|author={{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}}}

== Outside of Japan ==
A generic "Kunitama" was among the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name="koji-2010a">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53–54}} They are all [[Kunitsukami]] or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

This started in the [[Matsumae Domain]] during ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'' where many shrines in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] were forced to adopt such deities in that group. There was very little worship of such deities there at that time and as a result not much objection to it.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|394}} This came to be later used in many overseas shrines to justify colonialism.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|53–54}}

In Korea Kunitama and Amaterasu were enshrined together.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|126}} as a pair at all nationally Ranked shrines.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|139}} The colonization of Korea marked the beginning of a shift frrom a meiji era "pioneer theology" to a universal theology and Amaterasu became more prominent and was generally paired with Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|217}}

=== In Korea ===
Some people identified [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the government not wanting to take a stand on this enshrined the generic Okunitama at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] so believers could have their own interpretations.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}} {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q29410596}} was a strong advocate of these positions and his advocacy was associated with the enshrinement of Okunitama at both [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]], and [[Keijō Shrine]].<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|56}} He advocated enshrining of [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]] at {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|qid=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine|1=Chōsen Shrine}}, and others argued that in Korea Kunitama was Dangun and should be called Chosen kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|132}}

In 1936 [[Keijō Shrine]] released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title forr any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|140}}

An ethnic Korean group proposed to take over Okunitama worship after the war but was denied.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|57}}

State authorities at [[:en:Chōsen Jingu|Chōsen Jingu]] however never allowed for Okunitama to be called "Chosen kunitama" and indigenous Dangun traditions were suppressed in favor of worshipping Amaterasu in the shrine.<ref name="koji-2010a" />{{rp|54}}

=== Other areas ===
In {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|qid=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo|1=Manchukuo}} there were proposals to identify Kunitama with {{ill|Nurhaci|en|Nurhaci|qid=Q311189|lt=Nurhaci|1=Nurhaci}} but they were not accepted.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|161}}

At Mōkyō Jinja {{ill|Genghis Khan|en|Genghis Khan|qid=Q720|lt=Genghis Khan|1=Genghis Khan}} was venerated as Kunitama.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|175}}

In {{ill|Brazil|en|Brazil|qid=Q155|lt=Brazil|1=Brazil}} in a Japanese settlement a shrine named Bogure Jinja was created and worshipped Kunitama, identified with indigenous people of the area in a Kofun.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|209}}
